result:
The first thing I notice when I wake is the color of the light, a diluted peach that means the sun has climbed high enough to bounce off the apartment building across the alley but not yet high enough to slap the pavement. I keep my eyes closed for a while, pretending that the shade of the light is a place I could walk into, the way children think you can step through a mirror if you’re quick enough. Nothing about the day has been decided yet; the hours are still soft, like wax left too close to a radiator. I could choose anything. I could stay here until my back grows moss. Instead I swing my legs over the edge of the mattress and feel the familiar sting of the cold floor against my soles, a small pain that proves I’m still enrolled in the world.

When I was little I believed every adult had a secret map folded up in their sock drawer. On the map was the single route that would make sense of everything—why we moved so often, why my father’s laugh sounded like a rusted hinge, why my mother stared at the kitchen clock as if it might suddenly run backward. I assumed that at a predetermined age—twenty-five? thirty?—you were handed the key to the drawer along with a driver’s license and a credit card. The revelation never arrived. Instead I accumulated years the way a bookshelf accumulates dust: first a film you can wipe away, then a gray fur that works into the grain, finally something so ingrained you stop seeing it. One day I opened the drawer and realized the map had either never existed or I had mistaken the空白 spaces for roads.

I make coffee the way a retired watchmaker might disassemble a 1959 Omega: every motion rehearsed, unnecessary sound avoided. The kettle clicks, the grinder snarls, the water blooms the grounds into a dark planet. While it steeps I lean against the counter and listen to the building protest as it warms. Pipes sigh, floorboards flex like piano wires, someone two floors up drops something that rolls to a stop behind my ceiling. A century ago this place was a corset factory; before that, a stable for streetcars. I like to imagine the layers: horses stamping in the dark, women in shirtwaists tightening stays, now me in sweatpants waiting for caffeine to knit my thoughts together. History is only ever a laminated menu of wrong turns, but late at night you can still smell hay and singed cotton if you try.

The first cup tastes like wet pennies and burnt cedar. I drink it anyway, standing at the window that looks onto the airshaft. Between the opposing walls there is exactly enough room for one fat raccoon, three trash cans, and the idea of vertigo. Every spring a couple of sparrows attempts to nest on the sill, fails, tries again. Their persistence is hilarious and holy. I have watched them ferry twigs longer than their bodies, cram them into cracks no wider than a lie, then scream at squirrels who treat the construction site like a buffet. By July the ledge is littered with string, dental floss, once a child’s barrette shaped like a star. The birds vanish overnight, leaving the debris as proof that trying is its own country, even if you never receive a passport.

I should open my laptop and answer an email that begins “Hope this finds you well,” which is code for “You owe me something.” Instead I pick up a book I’ve already read six times: Taxi Driver Wisdom, a little square thing printed on blotter-thick paper, each page a single sentence supposedly overheard in the back of a cab. “Sometimes the only way out of the fire is through the wallet.” “Never trust a man who polishes his own shoes.” “The mirror is the only passenger who never tips.” Reading it is like chewing ice—it does nothing nutritive but clarifies the temperature inside you. Today the sentence that stops me is: “Everyone’s in a hurry to name the thing that’s eating them.” I close the book and feel something nameless gnaw my rib, a sensation exactly the shape of a Tuesday.

At ten-thirty I put on real pants—jeans with a button, the formal equivalent of a tuxedo in pandemic years—and walk to the pharmacy to buy nothing in particular. The city outside feels like it’s been unplugged and plugged back in; traffic lights blink, a bus exhales, but something in the circuitry hums differently. Masks have become chin decorations, yet people still flinch when someone coughs, a small bow to the god of almost-over. Inside the pharmacy the fluorescent tubes hum a note that makes my molars itch. I pace the aisles slowly enough to qualify as loitering. There is a sale on inexplicable objects: avocado-shaped bath sponges, diet pills that promise “radical self-trust,” phone chargers wrapped in plastic sturdy enough to skin a knee. I buy a bar of soap that claims to smell like yuzu and “liminal spaces.” At the register the teenager scanning items has the deadpan glamour of someone who understands she is temporarily invincible. Her eyeliner wings could slice bread. I want to tell her that nothing she’s worried about will matter in five years, but that’s the kind of lie only the old are licensed to sell, and I’m not sure I qualify.

Back on the sidewalk I realize I’ve walked the exact route my mother took every afternoon when she was dying, though she died somewhere else. The brain stores maps like scars, white ridges you can trace when the weather changes. She used to stop at the corner donut shop for decaf and a cruller she never finished, then sit on the bench outside the shoe-repair place that always smelled of acetone and leather soup. The bench is gone, replaced by a bike rack shaped like a hedgehog. I straddle one of the spikes and unwrap the soap, inhale. It smells like a grapefruit having an identity crisis. A woman walks past walking a dog the size of a mailbox; the dog is wearing a tutu and the expression of someone who has read the script and knows how it ends. The woman’s T-shirt says ASK ME ABOUT MY BRILLIANT FAILURE. I almost do, but the light changes and they trot away, late for another appointment with futility.

By noon the sun has perfected its aim. I weave through side streets where the houses shrink like ashamed heirs, their once-proud porches sagging under the weight of vinyl siding. On one tiny lawn an inflatable Santa has collapsed into a puddle of red nylon; it heaves slightly in the breeze, attempting, eight months early, to resurrect. I remember reading that carnival workers used to call the day-after teardown “the murder of joy.” That phrase returns to me whenever I see Christmas decorations out of season, as if festivity can die by calendar alone. I want to rescue the Santa, prop him up, hand him a lemonade, tell him the world is tough but not yet terminal. Instead I walk on, because kindness is sometimes heavier than apathy, and I haven’t had lunch.

I decide hunger is a kind of weather and duck into the diner that never carded me when I was underage and pretending to be a poet. laminated menus dangle above the counter like iced tongues. The waitress, Darlene, has been here since the Eisenhower administration; her name tag still bears the optimistic exclamation point she added during the Bicentennial. She doesn’t recognize me—why would she?—but she calls me “hon” with the fluency of someone who understands all strangers are just relatives between incarnations. I order eggs over easy and rye toast, the national breakfast of retroactive orphans. While I wait I eavesdrop on two men in hardhats debating whether birds have regional accents. One claims the pigeons in Boston sound more educated; the other swears Atlanta crows speak in hymns. Their earnestness makes my throat ache. I want to join them, offer the fact that Swedish nightingales have incorporated the beeps of reversing garbage trucks into their songs, but Darlene slides the plate in front of me and the moment closes like a faulty pocketknife.

The yolks are perfect suns. I pierce one with the corner of toast and watch it bleed neon across the white plate, a miniature apocalypse. Some people lose faith in humanity; I lose faith in breakfast, then find it again every time the diner’s industrial toaster spits out bread the color of late August. I salt the yolk, watch crystals dissolve like tiny white cities, and think about how every seven years we are technically a new person because cells replace themselves, yet the scar on my left knee from a 1999 skateboard crash remains stubbornly legible. Maybe memory lives outside the body, curled around lampposts, nested in diner upholstery, waiting to ambush you with the smell of burnt rye.

I tip Darlene forty percent because math is easier than gratitude, and step back into the afternoon, which has thickened like soup. Clouds the color of pencil erasers scrub the sky. I head toward the river because water is the only therapist that never bills you, though it occasionally claims collateral. On the way I pass the vacant lot where a developer promised affordable housing then vanished with the down payments; now it’s a meadow of weeds so tall they could audition for a fairytale. In the middle someone has erected a teepee of shopping carts, silver and glinting like a monument to consumerist withdrawal. A skinny man in a shredded tuxedo jacket stands beside it, conducting an invisible orchestra. As I approach he finishes the movement with a flourish, bows deeply, and says, “Mozart died of popcorn lung, pass it on.” I salute him, because that’s the only liturgy left.

The river is browner than yesterday, swollen from storms that happened somewhere else. I sit on the concrete embankment and watch a plastic bag drift downstream like a jellyfish that lost its faith. Across the water the old factory district has been converted to lofts with names like The Forge and Rivet House, their brick facades sandblasted until they look embarrassed about their past. A neon sign on the rooftop bar flashes HAPPY NEVER AFTER in pink script, a joke recycled from a thousand bachelorette parties. I try to remember when irony replaced oxygen, but the timeline slips. My phone buzzes: a text from someone I once kissed on a ferris wheel during a thunderstorm. The message reads, Just heard our song on the grocery store speaker and thought of you. Hope you’re thriving. The emoji that follows is a sunflower, which either means forgiveness or sarcasm; with her you needed an advanced degree in semiotics. I type and delete seventeen replies, finally settling on the waving-hand emoji, the digital equivalent of smiling through a screen door.

A jogger pounds past wearing headphones bright enough to land aircraft. The bass leaks: untz untz untz, the heartbeat of a future whose main export is fatigue. I wonder what playlist accompanies his inner monologue, whether he’s imagining himself in a montage that ends with him vomiting triumphantly at the finish line. I’ve never understood the physics of running for fun; it’s like paying rent to occupy tomorrow’s soreness. Still, I envy the clarity of sweat, the way it salts the skin and signals effort. My own exertions are largely invisible—thoughts doing push-ups behind the curtain, emotions running laps in the basement.

I stay until the sky decides on rain, a polite drizzle at first, like the universe is testing the temperature. Then it commits, and the river’s surface stipples into a million nail heads. I walk home slowly, letting the water find the parts of my hair that still remember childhood cowlicks. Umbrellas bloom around me, a funeral procession for dryness. Someone’s abandoned newspaper clings to the sidewalk; the headline promises THE END OF HISTORY, a recycling bin of prophecy. I step over it and watch black ink bleed into the gutter, forming Rorschach blots of yesterday’s catastrophes. One looks exactly like my father’s profile if he’d lived to see tonight; another resembles the dog in the tutu, now victorious.

Inside again, I peel off wet clothes and stand naked in the middle of the living room, a slightly ridiculous monument to evaporation. The apartment smells of coffee grounds and the ghost of the yuzu soap. I open the box, hold the bar to my nose, and get nothing—scent locked behind a wax paper border. Maybe that’s what liminality smells like: the moment before release. I place the soap on the windowsill, where tomorrow’s light will teach it how to behave.

Evening arrives sideways, a bruise-colored light that makes the walls look like they’re remembering a previous tenant. I cook pasta in the shape of radiators, toss it with butter and the last of the parmesan, which has hardened into geological strata. While it melts I play a record I found at a yard sale: a language-learning LP titled “Portuguese for Loners.” The narrator’s voice is buttery, relentlessly optimistic. “In Lisbon, the evenings stretch like taffy,” he intones, as if that solves anything. I repeat the phrase out loud, my accent sliding off the syllables like a drunk on ice. The pasta is perfect al dente, a small miracle I catalog next to the sparrows and Darlene’s yolks.

After dinner I sit at the typewriter I bought during a brief phase when I believed machinery could save me from metaphor. The ribbon is so dry it tattoos ghosts, but I peck anyway: THE MAP WAS THE SCAR. THE SCAR WAS THE MIRROR. THE MIRROR WAS THE FIRE. I tear the page out, crumple it, then smooth it again, because wastepaper baskets are just mouths that never digest. On the wall above the desk hangs a photograph of my mother at twenty-two, standing beside a car whose bumper reads NO DEPOSIT, NO RETURN. She’s wearing sunglasses shaped like exclamation points, and her smile is so confident it looks photoshopped by history. I tell her about the inflatable Santa, the Mozart prophet, the dog in the tutu. She doesn’t answer, but the picture buckles slightly when the heat kicks on, a shrug or a warning.

Midnight finds me on the roof, wrapped in a blanket that smells of campfire and someone else’s heartbreak. The rain has paused, leaving the sky scrubbed and star-pocked. City glow eats most constellations, but Orion still muscles through, his belt cinched tight against urban expansion. I search for satellites, those polite robots that wave as they pass. One blinks red, then vanishes into the haze above Newark; I decide it’s carrying tonight’s secrets to a server farm where they will be freeze-dried and sold back to me as targeted ads. The air smells of wet tar and the exhausted lilacs in the neighbor’s planter. Somewhere below, a siren Dopplers out, a sound shaped like a question no one answers.

I think about the email I avoided, the song in the grocery store, the sentence in the taxi book. Everyone’s in a hurry to name the thing that’s eating them, but maybe the thing enjoys anonymity, prefers to dine in the dark. Maybe giving it a name only encourages it to order dessert. I try to feel inside myself for the edges of the creature, but it’s like groping for the end of a dream—every time I close in, the shape subsides and reforms as the absence of shape. All I know is that it’s patient, punctual, and has my eyes.

Eventually the cold wins. I climb back down the ladder, lock the roof door, and return to the apartment where the typewriter waits like a interrogation lamp. I feed it a fresh sheet, roll it until the paper nurses its wound. Then I write the only thing that feels true: Tomorrow the light will be peach again, and I will pretend I can walk into it. The page jerks upward, the bell dings, a small silver verdict. I leave the sentence there, a flag no wind can read, and go to brush my teeth.

In the bathroom mirror my reflection looks surprised to still exist, eyes ringed with the sort of fatigue that could be mistaken for wisdom at a distance. I open the medicine cabinet, half expecting to find the secret map folded between expired ibuprofen and the spare razor. Instead there’s only the usual debris: a ticket stub from a concert that felt like revelation, a plastic dinosaur my nephew insisted I keep, a note in my own handwriting that says YOU ARE HERE, an arrow pointing nowhere in particular. I close the door, spit mint into the sink, and watch the foam spiral down the drain, a galaxy collapsing politely.

Back in bed I lie very still, trying to hear the sparrows rustle on the sill, but they’ve learned to sleep without applause. The city dims, light by light, until only the red gaze of the smoke detector remains, a single electric coal winking in the dark. I imagine it spelling Morse code: NOT YET NOT YET. Fine, I think, and pull the blanket higher, preparing to reenter the version of myself who dreams in chapters, who wakes already halfway translated into tomorrow’s stranger. Outside, the inflatable Santa probably reinflates at dawn, hungry for another round of hope. Inside, I catalog the day’s small salvations: yolk, soap, Orion’s belt, the stranger’s lunatic symphony. They feel like enough, though I know hunger resets by morning. Still, tonight the thing with no name curls up at the foot of the bed, temporarily sated, and I let it. We negotiate a fragile truce: I won’t expose it, and it won’t finish me before the light turns peach again. Somewhere between that promise and the first drop of rain against the pane, sleep arrives—not a map, not a scar, just the next blank page rolling into the machine, waiting for the key to strike.

Outside, the inflatable Santa probably reinflates at dawn, hungry for another round of hope. Inside, I catalog the day’s small salvations: yolk, soap, Orion’s belt, the stranger’s lunatic symphony. They feel like enough, though I know hunger resets by morning. Still, tonight the thing with no name curls up at the foot of the bed, temporarily sated, and I let it. We negotiate a fragile truce: I won’t expose it, and it won’t finish me before the light turns peach again. Somewhere between that promise and the first drop of rain against the pane, sleep arrives—not a map, not a scar, just the next blank page rolling into the machine, waiting for the key to strike.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
